ncert.chapter: Introduction: Tracing Changes  through a Thousand Years

sections.label: Chapter-end
sections.html:
Q 1

Who was considered a Foreigner in the past?

Solution

The term ‘Foreigner’ is used to refer to any stranger who appeared in any village. The words ‘Pardesi’ and ‘Ajnabi’ were synonymous with the word Foreigner. These words were used by citizens of that place or village to refer to any person who arrived for some work for a short time and who was not a part of that society or culture.

Q 2

We do not find inscriptions for the period after 700.

Q 3

The Marathas asserted their political importance during the period between 700 and 1750 CE.

Q 4

Forest-dwellers were sometimes pushed out of their lands with the spread of agricultural settlements.

Q 5

Sultan Ghiyasuddin Balban controlled Assam, Manipur and Kashmir.

Q 6

Archives are places where ________ are kept.

Q 7

________ was a 14th-century chronicler.

Q 8

________, ________, ________, ________ and ________ were some of the new crops introduced into the subcontinent during this period.

Solution

Potatoes, Corn, Chillies, Tea, and Coffee.

Q 9

List some of the technological changes associated with this period.

Solution

Following are some of the technological changes that were associated with this period:

  1. The use of Persian wheel in irrigation.
  2. The use of spinning wheel in weaving.
  3. The use of firearms in combat.
Q 10

What were some of the major religious developments during this period?

Solution

During this period important changes occurred in the Hindu religion. The Hindus started worshipping new deities. The construction of temples by royalty, and the growing importance of Brahmanas, the priests were other developments of this period. Brahmanas' importance grew due to their knowledge of Sanskrit language. They were patronized by the Emperors. The idea of bhakti emerged among people. The merchants and migrants brought with them the teachings of Quran, the holy book of Muslims.

Q 11

In what ways has the meaning of the term 'Hindustan' changed over the centuries?

Solution

The term 'Hindustan' has changed over the centuries in the following ways:

  1. For the first time, in the 13th century, Minhaj-i-Siraj used the term ‘Hindustan’. By ‘Hindustan’ he meant the areas of Punjab, Haryana and the land between Ganga and Yamuna rivers.
  2. The poet Amir Khusrau used this term in his poems in the 14th century.
  3. In the 16th century, Babur used the term to describe the geography, the fauna and the culture of the inhabitants of the subcontinent.
  4. Important point is that the term ‘Hindustan’ did not carry the political and national meanings as we use it today.
Q 12

How were the affairs of Jatis regulated?

Solution

The affairs of Jati were regulated in the following ways

  1. Jatis were grouped or divided as per their family backgrounds and occupations that their families carried on. People had to accept these rules.
  2. Jatis were the sub-castes as per the power, influence and resources controlled by members of the Jati.
  3. An assembly or group of elders was responsible for enforcing these regulations and were called as Jati Panchayat.
  4. However, the Jati or caste of one area varies from that of other areas in many aspects.
Q 13

What does the term pan-regional empire mean?

Solution

The term pan-regional empire means an empire spanning diverse regions.  The dynasties like Cholas, Khaljis, Tughluqs and Mughals extended their empires pan-regional. Though, not all these empires were equally stable or successful. But pan-regional rule altered the character of the regions. Most of the regions across the subcontinent were left with the legacies of the big and small states that had ruled over them. The emergence of many distinct and shared traditions in governance the economy elite cultures and languages were some of the prominent factors that took place as a result of pan-regional rules.

Q 14

What are the difficulties historians face in using manuscripts?

Solution

In early days manuscripts were handwritten. They used palm leaves which was difficult to maintain. The later paper became cheaper and more widely available. People used it to write holy texts, chronicles of rulers, letters and teachings of saints, etc. There was no printing press in those days, so scribes copied manuscripts by hand. As scribes copied manuscripts, sometimes they introduced changes according to their own. So, the different manuscripts of the same text became substantially different from one another over the centuries. Historians have to read the different versions and guess what the author had originally written.

Q 15

How do historians divide the past into periods? Do they face any problems in doing so?

Solution

For any historians, time is not just passing of hours, days or years. Instead, it is the reflection of social, economic, cultural and religious changes that occur through the ages. Division of past events, happenings into large segments that is periods, make it easy to study time for any historians. British historians divided the history of India into three periods: Hindu, Muslim and British. This division was based on the idea that the religion of rulers was the only important historical change. But this division ignored the rich diversity of the Indian subcontinent. Most historians take social and economic factors into account in order to characterise the major elements of different moments of the past. Historians thus faced difficulties to describe the entire period as one historical unit.

Q 16

Compare either Map 1 or Map 2 with the present-day map of the subcontinent, listing as many similarities and differences as you can find.
Map 1

Map 2

Solution

Map 1 was made in 1154 CE by al-Idrisi, an Arab geographer. This section is a detail of the Indian subcontinent from his larger map of the world. Map 2 was made by a French cartographer in 1720. Both maps are quite different from each other, even though they represent the same area. In Map 1 we find south India at the place where we would expect to find north India and Sri Lanka is the island at the top. The place names are in Arabic. Some familiar places like Kanauj in Uttar Pradesh have been spelt as Qanauj. In comparison to this Map 2 was made nearly 600 later after Map 1.

Q 17

Find out where records are kept in your village or city. Who writes these records? Is there an archive? Who manages it? What kinds of documents are stored there? Who are the people who use it?

Solution

 In our village, records are kept in the Panchayat office. Gram Sevak who is a government employee writes these records. There is no archive in our village. Sarpanch manages it. The documents stored there include the record of the land of the village, data of births and deaths, the number of public properties, data of health centres, etc. Villagers use these records. The certificates are issued to the villagers on the basis of these records.

sections.label: Chapter-end
sections.html:
Q 1

Match the following :

(a) Gurjara-Pratiharas (i) Western Deccan 
(b) Rashtrakutas (ii) Bengal
(c) Palas (iii) Gujarat and Rajasthan 
(d) Cholas (iv) Tamil Nadu 
Solution

(a) - (iii), (b) - (i), (c) - (ii), (d) - (iv)

Q 2

Who were the parties involved in the tripartite struggle?

Solution

The parties involved in the tripartite struggle were Gurjara-Pratihara, Rashtrakuta and Pala dynasties.

Q 3

What were the qualifications necessary to become a member of a committee of the sabha in the Chola empire?

Solution

The prescribed qualifications necessary to become a member of a committee of the sabha in the Chola empire were:

  1. All those who wish to become members of the sabha should be owners of the land from which land revenue is collected.
  2. They should have their own homes.
  3. They should be between 35 and 70 years of age.
  4. They should have knowledge of the Vedas.
  5. They should be well-versed in administrative matters and honest.
  6. If anyone has been a member of any committee in the last three years, he cannot become a member of another committee.
  7. Anyone who has not submitted his accounts, as well as those of his relatives, cannot contest the elections.
Q 4

What were the two major cities under the control of the Chahamanas?

Solution

The two major cities under the control of the Chahamanas were Delhi and Ajmer.

Q 5

How did the Rashtrakutas become powerful?

Solution

Rashtrakutas become powerful in the following ways:

  1. In the beginning, Rashtrakutas were subordinated to the Chalukyas of Karnataka.
  2. In the mid-8th century Dantidurga, a Rashtrakuta chief, overthrew his Chalukya overlord.
  3. He performed a ritual called Hiranya - Garbha with the help of Brahmanas.
  4. This ritual was then, considered to lead the rebirth of the sacrificer as Kshatriya, even if he was not a Kshatriya born.
Q 6

What did the new dynasties do to gain acceptance?

Solution

The new dynasties undertook the following things to gain acceptance:

  1. The new dynasties were based in some specific regions.
  2. They tried to control other areas.
  3. The most prized area was the city of Kanauj in the Ganga valley.
  4. Rulers belonging to Gurjara-Pratiharas, Rashtrakutas and the Palas fought against each other to get control of Kanauj for centuries.
  5. Rulers also tried to demonstrate their power and resources by building large temples. So, when they attacked one another’s kingdoms, they often chose to target temples, which were sometimes extremely rich.
  6. One of the best known of such rulers is Sultan Mahmud of Ghazni, Afghanistan. He extended control over parts of Central Asia, Iran and the north-western part of the subcontinent. He raided the subcontinent almost every year – his targets were wealthy temples, including that of Somnath, Gujarat.
Q 7

What kind of irrigation works were developed in the Tamil region?

Solution

Agriculture had developed earlier in other parts of Tamil Nadu, it was only from the fifth or sixth century that this area was opened up for large-scale cultivation. A variety of new methods were discovered for irrigation. In many areas, wells were dug.
Huge tanks were constructed for rainwater storage. Sluice-gates were constructed to regulate the outflow of water from a tank into the channels that irrigated the fields. Forests were cleared, the land was levelled in other regions.  In the delta, region embankments had to be built to prevent flooding and canals had to be constructed to carry water to the fields. In many areas, two crops were grown in a year.

Q 8

What were the activities associated with Chola temples?

Solution

Multiple activities were associated with Chola temples:

  1. Chola temples often became the nuclei of settlements that grew around them. These were centres of craft production. Temples were also endowed with land by rulers as well as by others.
  2. Land grants were given to the temple by kings and others. The produce of such land went for the livelihood of priests, garland makers, cooks, sweepers, musicians, dancers, etc.
  3. Temples were not only places of worship but also the hub of economic, social and cultural life as well.
  4. Crafts making was carried on by craftsmen making bronze images of deities and devotees.
  5. Chola bronze images are considered amongst the finest in the world. While most images were of deities, sometimes images were made of devotees as well.
Q 9

Look at Map 1 once more and find out whether there were any kingdoms in the state in which you live. (New Kings and Kingdoms)

Solution

The answer may vary as per the students' state where they live. So the students should answer this question on the basis of their individual study of the Map.

Q 10

Contrast the elections in Uttaramerur with present day panchayat elections.

Solution

In Uttaramerur election the names of the persons eligible to be members of the committees were written on small tickets of palm leaves. The tickets were put into an earthenware pot. Thereafter a young boy was asked to take out the tickets, one by one for each committee. But the present-day panchayat members are elected through a general election The names of the total members are printed with a symbol allotted to them on a paper called ballot paper. The voter put a stamp over their choice and drops it in a ballot box. Every procedure is organized by a statutory body. Hence, in Uttaramerur, a lottery system was in practice to elect the members of panchayat. But the present-day panchayat elections don't use this system. 

Q 11

Compare the temple shown in this chapter with any present-day temple in your neighbourhood, highlighting any similarities and differences that you notice.

Solution

Do it yourself.

[The present-day temples do not have such intricate outer structure, except a few as shown in this chapter. Only Akshdhar Dham could match such outerwalls as that of the temple that at Gangaikondacholapuram.]

Q 12

Find out more about taxes that are collected at present. Are these in cash, kind, or labour services?

Solution

Today we pay a number of taxes like property tax, road tax, service tax, entertainment tax, water tax, etc. These taxes are in cash not in kind, nor in labour services.

sections.label: Chapter-end
sections.html:
Q 1

Which ruler first established his or her capital at Delhi?

Solution

Tomara Rajputs were the first rulers who established their capital at Delhi.

Q 2

What was the language of administration under the Delhi Sultans?

Solution

Persian was the language of administration under the Delhi Sultans.

Q 3

In whose reign did the Sultanate reach its farthest extent?

Solution

 The Sultanate reached its farthest extent in the reigns of Alauddin Khaiji and Muhammad Tughluq.

Q 4

From which country did Ibn Battuta travel to India?

Solution

Ibn Battuta belonged to Morocco, Africa.

Q 5

According to the circle of justice, why was it important for military commanders to keep the interests of the peasantry in mind?

Solution

Fakhr-i Mudabbir wrote in the thirteenth century about “circle of justice”. He opined that a king cannot survive without soldiers. And soldiers cannot live without salaries. Salaries come from the revenue collected from peasants. But peasants can pay revenue only when they are prosperous and happy. This happens when the king promotes justice and honest governance.

Q 6

What is meant by the internal and external frontiers of the Sultanate?

Solution

The internal frontier of the Sultanate: The Sultanate aimed at consolidating the hinterland of the garrison town.
External Frontier of the sultanate: It aims at the military expeditions of the Sultanate in Southern India which started during the reign of Alauddin Khalji and culminated with Muhammad Tughluq.

Q 7

What were the steps taken to ensure that muqtis performed their duties? Why do you think they may have wanted to defy the orders of the Sultans?

Solution

The following steps were taken to ensure that muqtis performed their duties:

  1. Control over muqtis was very highly effective only if their office was not inheritable and if they were assigned iqtas for a short time span and shifted regularly.
  2. Accountants were appointed by the state to check the amount of revenue collected by the muqtis. Muqtis collected only the taxes prescribed by the state and kept the required number of soldiers.
Q 8

Do you think the authors of tawarikh would provide information about the lives of ordinary men and women?

Solution

No, the authors of Tawarikh would provide information about the lives of ordinary people because:

  • The authors of Tawarikh lived in cities (mainly Delhi) and hardly ever in villages.
  • They often wrote their histories for Sultans in the hope of rich rewards.
  • These authors advised rulers on the need to preserve an “ideal” social order based on birthright and gender distinctions heir ideas were not shared by everybody.
Q 9

Raziyya Sultan was unique in the history of the Delhi Sultanate. Do you think women leaders are accepted more readily today?

Solution

Raziyya Sultan was unique in the history of the Delhi Sultanate because she is the first woman to become the ruler of the Sultanate. She was more able and qualified than all her brothers.
Yes, I think women leaders are accepted more readily today because of democracy. Now, the world has become an open-minded in terms of gender equality and would never mind having a woman as their leader. Many woman in this period proved that a woman too can be at the top of leaders. Moreover, people widely supported the woman as their leader.

Q 10

Why were the Delhi Sultans interested in cutting down forests? Does deforestation occur for the same reasons today?

Solution

The Delhi Sultans interested in cutting down forests to give land to the peasants and carry out agricultural practices. Also, new fortresses and towns were established to protect trade routes and to promote regional trade. They also cleared forests in order to safeguard their frontiers and quick movement of their army.
In today's world, deforestation occurs due to the construction of roads, railways, houses, and industries. So, we can say that deforestation occurs for the same reasons today only the perspective of views has been changed.

Q 11

Find out whether there are any buildings built by the Delhi Sultans in your area. Are there any other buildings in your area that were built between the twelfth and fifteenth centuries? Describe some of these buildings, and draw sketches of them.

Solution

Qutb Minar, Haus Khas, Fort of Firoz Shah Kotia, etc. are some of the buildings that are built by the Sultans of Delhi in my area (Delhi). Students are suggested to collect the photographs of these buildings and describe them in their own language.

sections.label: Chapter-end
sections.html:
Q 1

Match the following:

Column A Column B
(a) Mansab (i) Marwar
(b) Mongol (ii) Governor
(c) Sisodiya Rajput (iii) Uzbeg
(d) Rathor Rajput (iv) Mewar
(e) Nur Jahan (v) rank
  (vi) Jahangir
Solution

(a)-(v), (b)-(iii), (c)-(iv), (d)-(i), (e)-(vi), (f)-(ii)

Q 2

The five Deccan Sultanates were Berar, Khandesh, Ahmadnagar, _________ and ________.

Q 3

If Zat determined a Mansabdar's rank and salary, Sawar indicated his ________ responsibilities.

Q 4

Abul Fazl, Akbar’s friend and counsellor, helped him frame the idea of ________  so that he could govern a society composed of many religions, cultures and castes.

Q 5

What were the central provinces under the control of the Mughals?

Solution

The central provinces under the control of the Mughals were Delhi, Agra, Awadh, Allahabad, Bihar, Bengal, Gujarat, Ajmer, Lahore, Multan and Kabul.

Q 6

What was the relationship between the mansabdar and the jagir?

Solution

A Jagir was a revenue assignment for the Mansabdars. The Mansabdars had the right to collect revenue from a Jagir but they could not reside in or administer the jagir.

Q 7

What was the role of the zamindar in Mughal administration?

Solution

Zamindars were powerful local chieftains appointed by the Mughal rulers. Great influence and power were enjoyed by these zamindars. They collected taxes from the peasants and gave them to the Mughal Emperor. Thus, they played the role of intermediaries.

Q 8

How were the debates with religious scholars important in the formation of Akbar's ideas on governance?

Solution

Akbar interacted with people of different faiths that made him realise that a religious scholar who emphasised ritual and dogma were often bigots. Their teachings created divisions and disharmony amongst his subjects. This finally led Akbar to the idea of Sulh-i-Kul or universal peace. He inferred that the idea of tolerance was of utmost importance because it did not discriminate between people of different religions in his realm. Instead, it focussed on a system of ethics i.e. honesty, justice, peace. These were the virtues universally applicable. Finally Akbar, with the help of Abul Fazl, formed a vision of governance around the idea of Sulh-i-Kul.

Q 9

Why did the Mughals emphasise their Timurid and not their Mongol descent?

Solution

The Mughal rulers were descendants of two great lineages of rulers. From their mother’s side, they were descendants of Genghis Khan, ruler of the Mongol tribes. From their father’s side, they were the successors of Timur, the ruler of Iran, Iraq and modern-day Turkey.
However, the Mughals did not like to be called Mongol because Mongol’s especially Genghis Khan’s, memory was associated with the massacre of innumerable people. It was also linked with the Uzbegs, their Mongols competitors. On the other hand, the Mughals were proud of their Timurid ancestry, because Timur captured Delhi in 1398.

Q 10

How important was the income from land revenue to the stability of the Mughal Empire?

Solution

The land revenue was an important source of the income of the Mughal Empire. It was because the money collected as taxes was spent in maintaining a large army, building forts, purchasing weapons and fighting wars. Revenues earned by taxing lands was also spent in initiating welfare schemes for the poor. The monetary security provided by the money earned through taxing lands helped in the overall strengthening of the Mughal Empire and provided stability to it.

Q 11

Why was it important for the Mughals to recruit mansabdars from diverse backgrounds and not just Turanis and Iranis?

Solution

With the expansion Mughal empire, the Mughal’s need bodies of personnel, hence they recruit Mansabdars from diverse backgrounds and not just Turanis and Iranis but also Afghans, Rajputs, Marathas and other groups, It was important for the Mughal Emperor to recruit diverse bodies of people in order to make regional people comfortable with them.

Q 12

Like the Mughal Empire, India today is also made up of many social and cultural units. Does this pose a challenge to national integration?

Solution

No, not at all, this does not pose any challenge to national integration. Unity in diversity is a special feature of India. Indians may belong to different regions, cultures, castes, and creeds. But this does not signify that they all are different people. They are all one and are proud of being Indians. Our Constitution does not discriminate us on the basis of caste, religion, and sex. There is no official religion. At times, social conflicts erupt, but they are sorted out in an amicable way. Whenever there is an external threat, all Indians come together. The unity of India was seen during the freedom struggle, during external aggression were it is from Pakistan or China.

Q 13

Peasants were vital for the economy of the Mughal Empire. Do you think that they are as important today? Has the gap in the income between the rich and the poor in India changed a great deal from the period of the Mughals?

Solution

Agriculture plays a vital role in the Indian economy. Peasants are important today as they were during the Mughal Empire. They pay land revenue to the government which is used in various policies, grants, subsidies, projects. During the Mughal rule, all were virtually agriculturists. No other source of income was different from that of agriculture. So, the income gap between rich and poor was not so much as is today. Now, people find more income-based jobs other than agriculture. Still, peasants are important today as more than 33 percent of the population of our country is agriculturists. Agriculture and allied sectors like forestry and fisheries accounted for 13.7 percent of the GDP.

Q 14

The Mughal Empire left its impact on the different regions of the subcontinent in a variety of ways. Find out if it had any impact in the city, village or region in which you live.

Solution

I live in Delhi. Delhi was the capital of the Mughal empire. The Empire changed the face of the city. We see the Red Fort, Chandni Chowk, Jama Masjid, Humayun’s Tomb, the Mughal garden, etc. in the city. The city still looks like the ancient capital in the old Delhi area. People still feel proud to have their homes in such a capital city. People from all over the world come to see it. Delhi looks very colorful and significant with such a historical background.

sections.label: Chapter-end
sections.html:
Q 1

Match the following:

(a) Garh (i) Khel
(b) Tanda (ii) Chaurasi
(c) Labourer (iii) Caravan
(d) Clan (iv) Garha Katanga
(e) Sib Singh (v) Ahom state
(f) Durgawati (vi) Paik
Solution

(a)-(ii), (b)-(iii), (c)-(vi), (d)-(i), (e)-(v), (f)-(iv)

Q 2

The new castes emerging within varnas were called ________.

Q 3

________ were historical works written by the Ahoms.

Q 4

The ________ mentions that Garha Katanga had 70,000 villages.

Q 5

As tribal states became bigger and stronger they gave land grants to ________ and ________.

Q 6

Tribal societies had rich oral traditions.

Q 7

There were no tribal communities in the North-Western part of the subcontinent.

Q 8

The Chaurasi in Gond states contained several cities.

Q 9

The Bhils lived in the North-Eastern part of the subcontinent.

Q 10

What kinds of exchanges took place between nomadic pastoralists and settled agriculturists?

Solution

Wool, ghee, etc. were exchanged by Nomadic pastoralists with the settled agriculturists for grain, cloth, utensils and other products.

Q 11

How was the administration of the Ahom state organised?

Solution
  1. The Ahom state depended upon forced labour. Those forced to work for the state were known as Paiks. A census of the population was taken. Each of the villages had to send a number of paiks by rotation.
  2. People of densely populated areas were shifted to thinly populated areas. Ahom clans were thus broken up. By the first half of the 17th century, the administration became quite centralised.
  3. Almost all adult males served in the army during the war. At other times they are involved in building dams, irrigation system, and other public works.
  4. Ahom society was divided into class or Khels, A Khel controlled several villages. The peasant was given land by his village community, which even the king could not take it easy. Ahom's society was very sophisticated. Poets and scholars were encouraged and given land grants.
Q 12

What changes took place in Varna based society?

Solution

The changes in Varna based society took place on the basis of the following steps:

  1. In almost the entire subcontinent, society was already divided on the basis of Varna.
  2. Rules prescribed by the Brahmanas were accepted by the rulers of large kingdoms.
  3. The difference between the high and low and between the rich and the poor, increased.
  4. This further divided the hierarchy between social classes.
  5. With the growth of the economy and the needs of society, people with new skills were required.
  6. Smaller castes or jatis emerged within varnas.
  7. New castes among the Brahmanas emerged. Specialised artisans like-smiths, carpenters and masons were also recognised as separate jatis by the Brahmanas.
Q 13

How did tribal societies change after being organised into a state?

Solution

Changes in the society from tribal into organised state were remarkable in the following ways:

  1. The emergence of large states changed the nature of tribal society. Equal society gradually got divided into various unequal social classes.
  2. Brahmanas received land grants from the Gond rajas. They became more influential.
  3. The Gond chiefs now wished to be recognised as Rajputs.
  4. Aman Das, the Gond raja of Garha Katanga assumed the title of Sangram Shah. His son Dalpat, married princess Durgawati daughter of a Rajput King, who extended her kingdom.
  5. Despite the fall of Garha Katanga, the Gond kingdom survived for some time.
Q 14

Why were the Banjaras important for the economy?

Solution

The Banjaras were very important for the economy. They were trader-nomads and controlled trade and commerce. They played an important role in the transportation of grains to the city markets. They usually bought grain where it was cheaply available and carried it to places where it was dearer. They even transported foodgrain for the Mughal towns and during military campaigns.

Q 15

In what ways was the history of the Gonds different from that of the Ahoms? Were there any similarities?

Solution

Difference: The Gonds and the Ahoms had distinct historical trajectories. The Gonds, primarily residing in central India, were indigenous tribes known for their agricultural practices and cultural diversity. They had a decentralized social structure, lacking a unified political system. In contrast, the Ahoms, an ethnic group from Assam, had a more centralized political structure. They established the Ahom kingdom in the Brahmaputra Valley, adopting elements of the local Assamese culture while retaining their own distinct traditions. The Ahoms were known for their sophisticated military and administrative systems.

Similarity: Despite their differences, both communities shared similarities in their reliance on agriculture and the preservation of their unique cultural identities. Both groups also had to contend with external influences, adapting to changing political landscapes while striving to maintain their traditions.

Q 16

Plot the location of the tribes mentioned in this chapter on a map. For any two, discuss whether their mode of livelihood was suited to the geography and the environment of the area where they lived.

Solution

Several tribes live in different parts of India. See the map given below:

The two examples where geography suited their livelihoods are:

  1. Ahom settled in the northeastern part of the country. They experienced heavy rainfall. They introduced new methods of rice cultivation that required more water. They were engaged in building dams and irrigation systems.
  2. Bhils lived in western part of the country. It had fertile land and forests. By 16th century many of them led a settled life of agriculturists. Many remained hunter gathers.
Q 17

Find out about present-day government policies towards tribal populations and organize a discussion about these.

Solution
  1. Present-day government policies towards tribal populations are very positive.
  2. Education is being spread in the tribal areas.
  3. Facilities like roads, water, electricity have been increased.
  4. The government is doing activities for the overall upliftment of the tribal populations.
  5. The reservation policy of the government for the Schedule Tribe ensures them reserved seats in the field of education and government jobs.
  6. The government is also determined to protect its cultural and social traditions.
Q 18

Find out more about present-day nomadic pastoral groups in the subcontinent. What animals do they keep? Which are the areas frequented by these groups?

Solution

Present-day nomadic pastoral groups in the sub-continent are as follows:

Gaddi shepherds living in the western Himalayas, Gujjar Bakarwals, living in Jammu & Kashmir, Banjaras living in Rajasthan. These nomadic people keep sheep, goats, and camels. They frequently visit Kumaon region of Uttarakhand, Jammu & Kashmir and Rajasthan.

sections.label: Chapter-end
sections.html:
Q 1

Match the following:

(a) The Buddha (i) Namghar
(b) Shankaradeva (ii) Worship of Shiva
(c) Nizamuddin Auliya (iii) Questioned social differences
(d) Nayanars (iv) Sufi saint
(e) Alvars (v) Worship of Shiva
Solution

(a)-(iii), (b)-(i), (c)-(iv), (d)-(v), (e)-(ii)

Q 2

Shankara was an advocate of ________.

Q 3

Ramanuja was influenced by the ________.

Q 4

_______, ________and _______ were advocates of Virashaivism.

Solution

Basavanna, Allama Prabhu, Akkamahadevi

Q 5

________ was an important centre of the Bhakti tradition in Maharashtra.

Q 6

Describe the beliefs and practices of the Nathpanthis, Siddhas, and Yogis.

Solution
  1. According to them the path to salvation lay in mediation on the formless Ultimate Reality and the realisation of oneness with it.
  2. To achieve this they advocated intense training of the mind through meditation, body through yogasanas and exercises, etc.
  3. This made their group popular among ‘low’ castes.
  4. Their criticism of conventional religions, created the ground for devotional religion to become popular in Northen India.
Q 7

What were the major ideas expressed by Kabir? How did he express these?

Solution

Ideas of Kabir were based on a complete, indeed vehement and rejections of the major religious traditions. His teachings openly ridiculed all forms of external worship of both Brahmanical Hinduism and Islam. The teachings also included the pre-eminence of the priestly classes and the caste system. He believed in a formless Supreme God. Kabir expressed his ideas through poetry and bhajans. The language of his poetry was a form of spoken Hindi widely understood by ordinary people. Kabir’s ideas were known from a vast collection of verses called Sakhis and Pads said to have been composed by him and sung in form of bhajans.

Q 8

What were the major beliefs and practices of the Sufis?

Solution
  1. Sufis rejected religiosity and emphasised love and devotion to God, compassion towards all fellow human beings and respect for the creations of God.
  2. It was believed that the heart can be trained to look at the world differently and thus, elaborate methods of training were developed.
  3. They rejected idol worship and used considerably simplified rituals of worship into collective prayers (jamaat).
Q 9

Why do you think many teachers rejected prevalent religious beliefs and practices?

Solution
  1. Prevalent religious, beliefs and practices were rejected by many teachers. Some traditions were not good for society, e.g., women not treated with regard to men.
  2. Men are always treated well and women are treated as secondary.
  3. They believed that all human beings are not equal and this created hatred in society.
Q 10

What were the major teachings of Baba Guru Nanak?

Solution

The main teachings of Guru Nanak were:

  1. Worship of one God.
  2. Caste, creed or gender was irrelevant for attaining liberation.
  3. To pursue active life with a strong sense of social commitment.
  4. To use nam, dan and isnan for the essence as right worship, welfare of others and purity of conduct.
  5. To follow nam-japna, kirt-karna and vand-chhakna which highlights the importance of right belief and worship, honest living and helping others.
Q 11

For either the Virashaivas or the sants of Maharashtra, discuss their attitude towards caste.

Solution

The attitude of Virashaivas or the sants of Maharashtra towards caste was that:

  1. They followed a humanistic attitude.
  2. They believed in the equality of all human beings.
  3. They were against Brahmanical ideas about caste and the treatment of women.

Moreover, they believed that Bhakti means sharing other pains. They wished a society where people could live in harmony without any feelings of high and low, rich and poor.

Q 12

Why do you think ordinary people preserved the memory of Mirabai?

Solution

Ordinary people preserved the memory of Mirabai because:

  1. Though she was a Rajput princess married into the royal family of Mewar. But she had no interest in worldly affairs. Mirabai was devoted to Krishna and composed innumerable bhajans expressing her intense devotion.
  2. Her devotion to her deity was supreme. She lived a simple life with ordinary people.
  3. Her songs also openly challenged the norms of the ‘upper’ caste and became popular with the masses in Rajasthan and Gujarat.
Q 13

Find out whether in your neighbourhood there are any dargahs, gurudwaras or temples associated with saints of the bhakti tradition in your neighbourhood. Visit any one of these and describe what you see and hear.

Solution

Students are suggested to do this exercise themselves. Sample- There is a gurudwara in my neighbourhood. People who come hear worship the Guru Granth Sahib. On a daily basis, Shabad (words from the holy book) is sung.

Q 14

For any of the saint-poets whose compositions have been included in this chapter, find out more about their works, noting down other poems. Find out whether these are sung, how they are sung, and what the poets wrote about.
 

Solution

Sample for this question is as follows:
The dohas of sant kabirdas are still sung and are also included in the school syllabus. Kabir was a mystic poet and his poems (dohas) were in vernacular hindi. In his dohas, Kabir rejected the hypocrisy and misguided rituals in various religious practices of his day.

Q 15

There are several saint-poets whose names have been mentioned but their works have not been included in the chapter. Find out more about the language in which they composed, whether their compositions were sung, and what their compositions were about.

Solution

Sample for this question is as follows:
The Dohas of Sant kabirdas are still sung and are also included in the school syllabus. Kabir was a mystic poet and his poems (dohas) were in vernacular Hindi. In his dohas, Kabir rejected the hypocrisy and misguided rituals in various religious practices of his day.

sections.label: Chapter-end
sections.html:
Q 1

Match the following:

(a) Anantavarman (i) Kerala
(b) Jagannatha (ii) Bengal
(c) Mahodayapuram (iii) Orissa
(d) Mangalakavya (iv) Kangra
(e) Miniature (v) Kerala
(f) Lilatilakam (vi) Puri
Solution

(a) - (iii), (b) - (vi), (c) - (i), (d) - (ii), (e) - (iv), (f) - (v)

Q 2

What is Manipravalam? Name a book written in that language.

Solution

Manipravalam-literally means, “diamonds and corals” referring to the two languages, Sanskrit and the regional language (Malayalam). Lilatilakam is a text of Manipravalam. 

Q 3

Who were the major patrons of Kathak?

Solution

The major patrons of Kathak were Wajid Ali Shah the last Nawab of Awadh and the Mughal emperors.

Q 4

What are the important architectural features of the temples of Bengal?

Solution

Important architectural features of temples of Bengal are:

  1. Temples started to copy the double roofed or four roofed structure of the thatched huts.
  2. In the comparatively more complex four roofed structure, four triangular roofs placed on the four walls move up to converge on a curved line or a point.
Q 5

Why did Minstrels proclaim the achievements of heroes?

Solution

Minstrels used to recite poems and songs which depicted the stories of the Rajputs heroic deeds. By reciting such songs minstrels inspired others to follow the examples of Rajputs. Ordinary people were also attracted by these stories. These stories have great emotions, loyalties, friendship, love, valour, anger etc.

Q 6

Why do we know much more about the cultural practices of rulers than about those of ordinary people?

Solution

They adopted several methods to preserve their achievements. Stories about Rajput heroes were recorded in poems and songs, which were recited by specially trained minstrels. Rulers patronised highly skilled painters who primarily- illustrated manuscripts containing historical accounts and poetry. These were generally painted in brilliant colours and portrayed court scenes and other aspects of social life. Ordinary people did not do anything to preserve the records of their heroic deeds.  The above-mentioned reasons clearly show why we know more about the cultural practices of rulers than those of ordinary people.  

Q 7

Why did conquerors try to control the temple of Jagannatha at Puri?

Solution

Conquerors tried to gain control over the Jagannatha temple at Puri because it would make their rule acceptable to the local people since the temple was important as a place of pilgrimage. The temple was also a centre of authority in social and political matters.

Q 8

Why were temples built in Bengal?

Solution

Temples were built in Bengal because to increase in religious faith, Mosques had already been built, and powerful people wanted to demonstrate their power and proclaim their diety.

Q 9

Describe the most important features of the culture of your region, focusing on buildings, performing arts and painting.

Solution

The city of Delhi has buildings and monuments built by Mughal emperors, slave emperors, Rajput rulers, and many more. We have temples, mosques, and many other such places constructed by different rulers. Differences in cultures can still be seen in the areas of old Delhi and New Delhi. The Red Fort, Qutab Minar, India Gate, Parliament House, etc. have separate recognition.  Delhi is the centre where we can find all types of arts. Dances, plays etc. are performed very frequently. We can also find all types of paintings here.  

Q 10

Do you use different languages for (a) speaking, (b) reading, (c) writing? Find out about one major composition in language that you use and discuss why you find it interesting.

Solution
  1. For speaking, I use Hindi and Punjabi
  2. For reading, I use Hindi and English.
  3. For writing, I use Hindi and English.

Thus, I use different languages for speaking, reading and writing.

Q 11

Choose one state each from north, west, south, east and central India. For each of these, prepare a list of foods that are commonly consumed, highlighting any differences and similarities that you notice.

Solution
States List of Foods
Punjab (North) Makki ki roti, Sarason ka saag, Rajma-Chawal, Dal-chapatis
Rajasthan (West) Dalia, Dal and Churma, Pyaz Kachori
Kerala (South) Rice and fish, Idli-Sambhar, Dosa, Dhokla
West Bengal (East) Rice and fish
Madhya Pradesh (Central) Dal-chapatis, chawal-dal
Q 12

Choose another set of five states from each of these regions and prepare a list of clothes that are generally worn by women and men in each. Discuss your findings.

Solution
States Clothes worn by men Clothes worn by women
Jammu & Kashmir (North) pheran salwar
Karnataka dhoti davani
Andhra Pradesh dhoti kurta saree
West Bengal trouser-shirt saree
Rajasthan dhoti, pagadhi ghagra

sections.label: Chapter-end
sections.html:
Q 1

Nadir Shah invaded Bengal.

Q 2

Sawai Raja Jai Singh was the ruler of Indore.

Q 3

Guru Gobind Singh was the 10th Guru of the Sikhs.

Q 4

Poona became the capital of the Marathas in the 18th century.

Q 5

How were the Sikhs organised in the 18th century?

Solution

During the 17th century, the Sikhs got organised into a political community. This led to the regional state-building in Punjab.
Guru Gobind Singh fought many battles against the Rajputs as well as Mughal rulers, both before and after the institution of the Khalsa in 1699. After the death of Guru Gobind Singh in 1708, the Khalsa revolted against the Mughal authority under the leadership of Banda Bahadur and declared their sovereign rule. Banda Bahadur was captured in 1715 and executed in 1716. In the 18th century, the Sikhs organised themselves into a number of bands called Jathas and later on misls.
The Sikh under the Khalsa declared their sovereign rule and their territories in the late 17th century extended from the Indus to the Jamuna. Maharaja Ranjit Singh reunited the divided groups of Sikhs and established his capital at Lahore in 1799.

Q 6

Why did the Marathas want to expand beyond the Deccan?

Solution

Marathas wanted to expand beyond the Deccan because expansion brought enormous resources to them.

Q 7

Do you think merchants and bankers today have the kind of influence they had in the eighteenth century?

Solution

No, according to me the merchants and bankers today do not have the kind to influence which they had in the eighteenth century.

Q 8

Did any of the kingdoms mentioned in this chapter develop in your state? If so, in what ways do you think life in the state would have been different in the eighteenth century from what it is in the twenty-first century? (Eighteenth-Century Political Formations)

Solution

Do it yourself.
Hint: I live in Delhi. None of the Kingdoms mentioned in the chapter developed in this region. If any of the students live in any of the regions where the kingdoms mentioned in the chapter developed they should consult their history teacher and prepare their answer.

Q 9

Collect popular tales about-rulers from any one of the following groups of people: the Rajputs, Jats, Sikhs or Marathas.

Solution

Do it yourself.
